text: Isaac called Jacob, blessed him, and told him not to marry a Canaanite woman.
He sent him instead to Paddan-aram, to Laban's house, to find a wife among his mother's people.
Isaac prayed God Almighty would bless Jacob and multiply him into a company of peoples.
He asked God to give Jacob Abraham's blessing and the land he was living in as a sojourner.
So Isaac sent Jacob off to Laban, Rebekah's brother, in Paddan-aram.
Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him off to marry there, warning against Canaanite wives.
He saw Jacob obeyed his parents and went to Paddan-aram.
Esau realized Canaanite women displeased his father Isaac.
So Esau went to Ishmael and married his daughter Mahalath, in addition to his other wives.
Jacob left Beersheba and set out toward Haran.
He came to a certain place and stopped for the night, taking a stone for a pillow, and lay down to sleep.
He dreamed of a stairway set on the earth reaching to heaven, with angels going up and down on it.
The LORD stood above it and repeated to Jacob the promise of land given to Abraham and Isaac.
His offspring would be like dust, spreading in every direction, blessing all families of the earth.
God promised to be with Jacob, keep him, and bring him back to this land.
Jacob woke and said, surely the LORD is in this place, and I did not know it.
He was afraid and said this is none other than the house of God, the gate of heaven.
Early the next morning he set up his stone as a pillar and poured oil on top of it.
He named the place Bethel, though the city had been called Luz before.
Jacob made a vow: if God would be with him and keep him on this journey and bring him home safely,
so that he returned in peace to his father's house, then the LORD would be his God.
This stone would become God's house, and Jacob would give God a tenth of everything he received.
